environment.os: Windows 11 x64 (native — no WSL, no Docker)
environment.go: go1.26.5 windows/amd64 (official zip from go.dev, extracted to a temp GOROOT)
environment.gogc: 100, pinned in-process via debug.SetGCPercent(100) in both builds
environment.note: AVX-512 is load-bearing context, not incidental: Green Tea's scan path uses vector instructions on newer amd64 (Ice Lake / Zen 4+). This machine is therefore a best case. A pre-AVX-512 amd64 or an ARM64 machine was NOT measured.

subject.name: Green Tea garbage collector
subject.version: Go 1.26.5 (Green Tea is the DEFAULT GC as of Go 1.26, released 2026-02)
subject.invocation: default `go build` = Green Tea; `GOEXPERIMENT=nogreenteagc go build` = pre-1.26 classic GC
subject.freshness_note: The opt-out is build-time only and temporary — the Go 1.26 release notes say GOEXPERIMENT=nogreenteagc 'is expected to be removed in Go 1.27'. On Go 1.27+ this A/B is no longer buildable, so this run is only reproducible on a 1.26.x toolchain. Pin go1.26.5.

baseline.name: pre-1.26 classic Go GC
baseline.note: Same source, same toolchain, same machine — the only difference is the GC implementation compiled in.

why_this_experiment.note: Published reports disagreed. The Go team's own guidance is a 10-40% GC-overhead reduction 'in programs that heavily use the GC', tile38 reported ~35%, but DoltHub's 2025-09 experiment reported a roughly NEUTRAL result. This harness was designed to make that disagreement legible by sweeping one variable — heap POINTER DENSITY (fanout) — rather than by benchmarking one more application.

determinism.note: The workload is deterministic (fixed sizes, PRNG seeded 42, GOGC pinned). GCCPUFraction and wall time are NOT — they are timing measurements on a busy desktop OS. Cell values move run to run; the flat shape moves a LOT because its churn is only ~200ms and GCCPUFraction is a since-process-start cumulative. Compare the pattern across shapes, not the digits.

verification.method: run.sh re-executed on the same machine in SMOKE mode (live=100MB, churn=10, n=1) with a freshly downloaded go1.26.5, to check that the packaged harness still builds both variants and reproduces the reported direction.
verification.result: tree reproduced the headline exactly — GC CPU 11.63% (classic) -> 2.65% (green) = -77%, matching the recorded -77%; wall time -34.9%. graph reproduced the direction at -66% GC CPU (recorded: -70%).
verification.honest_note: At n=1 smoke scale the two noise-dominated cells behaved exactly as the recorded run's caveats predict: flat's GC CPU SIGN FLIPPED (0.34% -> 0.66%, i.e. 'worse'), and graph's wall time came out +17% despite its GC CPU falling by two thirds — both cells run only 2 GC cycles at this scale. This is evidence FOR the caveat, not against the finding: the paper claim is about pointer-dense heaps at n=5 medians, and the one cell with enough signal (tree) reproduced to the percentage point.

harness_provenance.note: Copied verbatim from the session workspace — this is the exact file compiled on 2026-07-19. No edits. results-raw.jsonl is the unmodified stdout of all 26 recorded runs (each line is one process); results.json contains the medians computed from it, so the summary is auditable against the raw file.

raw_artifacts.retention_policy: The temp GOROOT (go1.26.5 zip + extraction + build cache + binaries, ~500MB) was deleted after both the original run and the 2026-07-24 verification. Nothing else was produced.
raw_artifacts.note: results-raw.jsonl (26 lines, 4.7KB) IS the raw evidence and is committed here in full — there is no larger dump being withheld.
